coredata.dc:title: Investigating the effect of nitrogen and chlorine Co–doped carbon quantum dots in phenazine and quinoxaline sensitized solar cells

coredata.dc:creator: Jahanbani, Shabnam
Ghadari, Rahim
coredata.dc:description: We investigated five dyes for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) relying upon derivatives of phenazine and quinoxaline and also studied their optical characteristics and the influence of functional groups such as (-CH3, -NO2, -C = O, -CN, and -OH) on these characteristics. When the prepared dyes are directly used at DSSCs, 9-Nitrobenzo[a]phenazin-5-ol (3b) dye device showed an improved Jsc (0.288 mA cm−2), Voc (0.437 V), FF (0.446), and η (0.056 %) than the sensitized solar cells by other dyes. In the following, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot and nitrogen, chlorine co-doped carbon quantum dot are used to improve the performance of DSSCs. The results showed that the device sensitized using the sensitizer (5-hydroxybenzo[α]phenazin-9-yl)(phenyl) methanone (3c)/NCQD has the highest photovoltaic efficiency with Jsc (0.979 mA cm−2), Voc (0.465 V), FF (0.370), and η (0.168 %). The N and Cl co-doped carbon dots were synthesized with a green approach to improve the performance of solar cells by the co-sensitization method. This approach is improving the efficiency of DCSSs with a simple, eco-friendly, and low-cost technique. Among the prepared sensitizers, (5-hydroxybenzo[α]phenazin-9-yl)(phenyl) methanone (3c)/N, Cl-CQD demonstrates the highest photovoltaic efficiency with Jsc (1.31 mA cm−2), Voc (0.495 V), FF (0.466), and η (0.303 %). FT-IR, XRD, 13C NMR, 1H NMR, TEM, EDS-Map, AFM, DLS, and PL studies were used to confirm the structure of the synthesized dyes and quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Investigating the effect of nitrogen and chlorine Co–doped carbon quantum dots in phenazine and quinoxaline sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 5166616471 Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 5166616471 Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We investigated five dyes for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) relying upon derivatives of phenazine and quinoxaline and also studied their optical characteristics and the influence of functional groups such as (-CH3, -NO2, -C = O, -CN, and -OH) on these characteristics. When the prepared dyes are directly used at DSSCs, 9-Nitrobenzo[a]phenazin-5-ol (3b) dye device showed an improved Jsc (0.288 mA cm−2), Voc (0.437 V), FF (0.446), and η (0.056 %) than the sensitized solar cells by other dyes. In the following, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot and nitrogen, chlorine co-doped carbon quantum dot are used to improve the performance of DSSCs. The results showed that the device sensitized using the sensitizer (5-hydroxybenzo[α]phenazin-9-yl)(phenyl) methanone (3c)/NCQD has the highest photovoltaic efficiency with Jsc (0.979 mA cm−2), Voc (0.465 V), FF (0.370), and η (0.168 %). The N and Cl co-doped carbon dots were synthesized with a green approach to improve the performance of solar cells by the co-sensitization method. This approach is improving the efficiency of DCSSs with a simple, eco-friendly, and low-cost technique. Among the prepared sensitizers, (5-hydroxybenzo[α]phenazin-9-yl)(phenyl) methanone (3c)/N, Cl-CQD demonstrates the highest photovoltaic efficiency with Jsc (1.31 mA cm−2), Voc (0.495 V), FF (0.466), and η (0.303 %). FT-IR, XRD, 13C NMR, 1H NMR, TEM, EDS-Map, AFM, DLS, and PL studies were used to confirm the structure of the synthesized dyes and quantum dots.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Some data are in provided SI.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the beginning of the 21st century, humankind has been facing a lack of energy because the energy demand is increasing continually, which is one of the biggest problems. Therefore, developing renewable energy sources is the most significant demand of the century. We know the sun, as a green energy source, is the final source, of energy for our planet. Annually, the earth receives solar energy 3 × 1024 J [1,2]. Scientists aim to obtain ways to convert light energy to electricity with low manufacturing cost, good plasticity, assemble easy, environmentally-friendly facets, and tunable optical properties.

One of the suitable photovoltaic systems for this aim is dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The DSSCs, first offered by Grätzel and coworkers in 1991, provided a new field for developing photovoltaic devices [3–5]. The main components of DSSCs consist of a nanostructured semiconductor thin film impregnated with dye molecules, a redox electrolyte like iodide/triiodide redox couple to connect the electrodes, and a counter electrode for collecting electrons from an external circuit [6]. So far, the maximum DSSC power conversion efficiency reached ∼14 % [7]. In DSSCs, the dye plays a vital role in determining the efficiency of an efficient cell; its duties are light absorption, electron-hole separation, electron injection to the anode, and charge transfer. DSSCs fabricated with ruthenium-based dyes have high efficiency but these kinds of DSSCs are expensive. Therefore, using organic dyes has attracted more attention due to simple preparation and purification processes, abundant and cheap materials, and easily designable structures [8]. The design of organic dyes with the architecture of the donor-π bridge-acceptor (D-π-A) to be used in DSSC is a common procedure which is due to the creation of flexible and highly effective structures. Molecular orbital computations for these kinds of dyes have shown that the HOMO is located in the donor unit while the LUMO is concentrated in the acceptor group. The acceptor unit acts as an anchoring group, which leads to efficient electron injection from dye into TiO2. Organic dyes like porphyrins and phthalocyanines are electrochemically stable, superior light-harvesters, and have high extinction coefficients, interesting candidates as sensitizers for dye-sensitized solar cells [9,10].

Quantum dots are of two different kinds: organic quantum dots and inorganic quantum dots. Organic quantum dots are graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Organic quantum dots have good chemical stability, nice water dispersibility, high quantum yields, and excellent optical and electrical properties. The synthesis of this kind of nanostructures is simple and inexpensive. Different environmentally friendly synthesis methods are reported for these kinds of CQDs. CQDs are novel members of carbon-based graphitic nanostructures with sp2 hybridized, quasi/spherical shape and size beneath 10 nm [11,12].

Doped-CQDs and other fluorescent carbon nanomaterials can be synthesized easily by simple and green hydrothermal or pyrolysis methods. These methods are favorable because they are low-cost, nontoxic, and environmentally friendly. Because of CQD's unique properties, they are widely used for applications such as optics, drug carriers, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, and energy storage systems [11]. There are a variety of reports about CQD and doped CQD preparation using precursors like glutamic acid, fructose, glucose, citric acid, etc. Among these materials, citric acid is mostly used as a carbon source because it is a simple, inexpensive, water-soluble, green, and environment-friendly compound, so it is a good candidate for the preparation of doped-quantum dots such as NCQD [13].

The most important reason for the preparation of the NCQDs is the easy integration of nitrogen in carbon-based compounds because of the relatively similar atomic size of nitrogen with carbon and the formation of potent carbon-nitrogen double bonds. However, high electronegativity differences in N (χ=3.04) and C (χ=2.55) cause polarization in the NCQD structure and stimulate the electronic, optical, and magnetic properties. Therefore, the charge carrier density in NCQD with nitrogen insertion in the carbon structure increased. The cause of it is intense conjugation between the π-system of carbon-based materials and unpaired electrons of nitrogen atoms [14,15,18]. Wang et al. used NCQD as sensitizers in the quantum-dot-based solar cells (QDSCs), and they obtained the highest efficiency in the introduced QDSCs [16]. Reports show that NCQDs have been used to optimize the light absorbed by organic phthalocyanine dyes in DSSCs [13]. Graphene-based QDs (GQDs) are small in size graphene sheets (< 10 nm), with sp2 hybridized, and these compounds can be synthesized using (a) bottom-up approach of partial pyrolysis of organic material and (b) top-down approach from the modified Hummer's method [17]. Doped-CQDs have been employed in different DSSC components such as photoelectrode, electrolyte, sensitizer, and counter electrode (CE) [17].

Functionalized graphene quantum dots are a challenging research field for obtaining high-performance DSSC [17]. Pristine-CQD usually has a low quantum efficiency. To overcome this problem, heteroatom doping, especially nitrogen, can be a promising method to improve the optical properties and increase the quantum efficiency of CQDs [18]. Another method to improve these properties is the synthesis of co-doped CQDs with different heteroatoms [19]. Dong et al. used l-cysteine and citric acid (CA) as starting material, and a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method obtained N, S co-doped CQDs with a high quantum yield of 73 % [20]. Wang et al. reported that N, S co-doped graphene substances have superior electrocatalytic activity and are used as counter electrodes in DSSCs and QDSCs. That is because of the synergistic of nitrogen and sulfur heteroatoms [21]. Co-doped CQDs have been prepared with various methods, such as hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave digestion, electrochemical, and arc-discharge ones [18,20]. Products generated by these approaches have a low quantum yield, are expensive, and are not green [18]. Thus, providing an easy and low-cost method to produce products with high quantum yield and strong fluorescence emissions is very important [22].

According to the twelve principles of green chemistry, using green solvents in reactions is critical. Deep eutectic solvents (DESs) have emerged as novel green solvents. DESs can be formed with high purity by mixing two or more biodegradable, inexpensive, and nontoxic materials. These components can connect through hydrogen bonding between the hydrogen bond donor (HBD) and acceptor (HBA). Different methods are reported to synthesize CQDs in an eco-friendly way by using DESs as reaction media. Significantly, the synthesized N, Cl-CQDS have a high quantum yield and stability [23,24]. Therefore, considering the advantages mentioned for citric acid as a cheap and green substance, also because the proposed methods for synthesis of co-doped carbon quantum dots are usually non-green and expensive. As a result, DES is an optimal method to synthesize quantum dots with high efficiency and stability. Because DESs are a green solvent, they are a combination of safe, inexpensive, and biodegradable components.

According to the mentioned advantages, in this work, aromatic organic dyes based on quinoxaline and phenazine moieties were synthesized. Also, NCQDs with a cheap and green carbon source were synthesized. In the next step, the performance of the quinoxaline and phenazine-based DSSCs in the absence and presence of NCQDs have been analyzed. In continue, the nitrogen and chloride co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, Cl-CQDs) were synthesized with the use of urea (HBD) and choline chloride (HBA). In this case, the effect of Cl as a co-dopant on the performance of DSSCs can be studied.

4-Methyl-benzene-1,2-diamine (C7H10N2, 97 %), 4-nitro-1,2-phenylenediamine (C6H7N3O2, 97 %), 3,4-diaminobenzophenone (C13H12N2O, 97 %), o-phenylene-diamine (C6H8N2, 99.5 %), diaminomaleonitrile (C4H4N4, 98 %), 2‑hydroxy-1,4-naphtoquinone (lawsone) (C10H6O3, 97 %), citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5 %), urea (CH4N2O, 99 %), choline chloride (C5H14ClNO, 99 %), ammonia (25 %) hydrochloric acid (37 %), terpineol (C10H18O, 96 %), ethyl cellulose (48–49.5 %) and titan (IV) chloride (TiCl4, 99.9 %) were prepared from Sigma-Aldrich. Solvents including ethanol, acetic acid, acetone, and dimethylformamide, were purchased from Merck or Sigma-Aldrich. Fluorine-doped SnO2 (FTO) conductive substrates, standard iodine-based electrolytes, and platinum paste were prepared from Sharif Solar (IRASOL). Scotch tape and Surlyn adhesive were prepared by SUNLAB. Titanium (IV) oxide nanoparticles (TiO2, anatase, 10–25 nm, 99+ %) were prepared by US Research Nanomaterials, Inc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
Synthesis of nitrogen and chloride co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, cl-cqds)

ce:section.ce:para: The TiO2 pastes of reflector and transparent were prepared using TiO2 nanoparticles (20 and 300 nm), terpineol, acetic acid, and ethyl cellulose in ethanol under ultrasonic waves. The FTO conductive substrates were cleaned with deionized water, a solution of (0.1 M /HCl), acetone, and ethanol for 30 min in an ultrasonic bath and were dried under a stream of nitrogen gas before use. The cleaned substrates were soaked in TiCl4 solution (40 mM) at 70 °C for 30 min to form a (c-TiO2) layer. Substrates were washed with deionized water and ethanol and then dried. TiO2 viscous pastes of 20 nm (as a transparent layer) and 300 nm (as a reflector layer) were printed on a treated glass substrate using the doctor blade method and dried at 25 °C for 15 min. Afterward, the prepared anodes were annealed at 500 °C for 30 min in a muffle furnace under airflow. At this point, the annealed TiO2 layers were treated for the second time with a TiCl4 solution (40 mM) at 70 °C for 30 min. Substrates were washed with deionized water and ethanol and then dried. The treated substrates were cooled and then immersed in a dye solution (0.5 mM/DMF) for 16 h until the dye was sufficiently absorbed by the TiO2 nanoparticles [13,26].

The counter electrode was prepared using Pt paste coated on a cleaned (FTO) glass substrate with a drilled hole. Pt paste was printed on the conductive substrate by the doctor-blading method. The Pt substrate was annealed at 500 °C for 30 min. The separating working electrode from the counter electrode was accomplished using a Surlyn thermoplastic spacer of 50 mm thickness and 0.25 cm2 area. FTO glass covered with TiO2 nanoparticle paste was used as the working electrode, and FTO glass coated with Pt paste as the counter electrode. The heat was applied for 120 s to the prepared electrode to seal it. Electrolytes based on acetonitrile solvent were introduced between the cell, and they included 0.1 M lithium iodide, 0.5 M 4-tertbutylpyridine, 0.6 M 1,2-dimethyl-3-propylimidazolium iodide and 0.05 M iodine. In the end, the holes were closed using a fragment of the Surlyn spacer to prevent the electrolyte from escaping [7,13].

The FT-IR spectra of synthesized compounds were recorded in the 400–4000 cm−1 range using a Bruker company device (TENSOR 27 model, Germany). The 1H NMR spectra of dyes were recorded using a nuclear magnetic resonance device (Spectrospin Avance 400 MHZ model) made by Bruker company with (DMSO‑d6) as solvent. The 13C NMR spectrum was recorded using a 250 MHZ instrument at (DMSO‑d6) as the solvent and made by Bruker Company, Germany. The optical absorption properties of the NCQD, N, Cl-CQD, and synthesized dyes were recorded using a (Shimadzu UV-2100 model) spectrophotometer. The fluorescence property of the samples was recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer device (LS45 model) manufactured by PerkinElmer. A Tongda (TD-3700 model, China) device was used to record the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of samples in the scan range (2θ= 10° to 80°). The scanning electron microscope device (MIRA3 FEG-SEM model), is made by the Tescan company in Czechia. The TEM characterization was done with a Zeiss, Germany (LEO 906) transmission electron microscope at a 100 kV acceleration voltage. The AFM images of quantum dots with the (Nanosurf Mobile S model) and the manufacturing of the Switzerland-based Nanosurf company were registered. To record the particle size distribution of the samples, the Nanotrac wave model was used to dynamically light scatter the objects. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) of the samples was recorded by the electrochemical device (AUTO LAB/PGSTAT100 model) in a three-electrode cell containing glass carbon as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, platinum foil as the counter-electrode, and lithium perchlorate as the electrolyte. The DSSC's photovoltaic properties were measured on a solar simulator instrument (Fan Daghigh Kosar, SIM2002 model) equipped with a xenon lamp (300 W) and an air mass of 1.5. The power density of the simulated light was calibrated to (100 mW cm−2) using a typical silicon solar cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR analysis study of synthesized dyes and doped-carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The FT-IR spectra of dyes and synthesized carbon quantum dots are shown in Fig. 1
(a, b), respectively. In the spectrum of dye 3a, the stretching vibration bands of -C-H (aliphatic), -C꞊N, -C-O (phenol), and -O-H are detected at 2921.1, 1631.5, 1230.5, and 3434.4 cm−1, respectively. Stretching vibrations of the aromatic ring are measured at 1597.2 and 1532.9 cm−1 [25]. Stretching vibration bands of -C꞊N, -C-O (phenol), =CH (aromatic), and –OH of dye 3b are shown at 1629.0, 1220.7, 3079.4, and 3432.0 cm−1, respectively. The stretching vibrations of the aromatic ring are in the range of 1528.2 and 1594.0 cm−1. The dye 3b strong peaks in the region 1342.2 and 1528.2 cm−1 are related to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the nitro group, respectively [25]. In the FT-IR spectrum of dye 3c, stretching vibration bands of =CH (aromatic), -C꞊N, -C-O (phenol), and –OH are at 3066.4, 1654.7, 1224.8, and 3430.6 cm−1 frequencies, respectively. The vibrational frequency in 1654.7 cm−1 is related to the ketone carbonyl group, and conjugation causes a decrease in the vibrational frequency from 1715.0 to 1654. 7 cm−1. The peaks associated with the imine bond and the carbonyl group overlap in the range of 1654.7 cm−1. Also, stretching vibrations of the aromatic ring show pair peaks in the range of 1532.9 and 1594.3 cm−1. In the FT-IR spectrum of dye 3d, frequencies related to stretching vibrations of ꞊CH (aromatic), -C-O (phenol), -C꞊N, and -O-H are exhibited at 3062.8, 1225.3, 1630.1, and 3434.5 cm−1, respectively. The vibrations of the aromatic ring are in the range of 1593.3 and 1533.4 cm−1. In the FT-IR spectrum of dye 3e, the bands stretching vibrations of =CH (aromatic), -C-O (phenol), and -O-H are shown at 3075.9, 1233.9, and 3344.8 cm−1, respectively. The aromatic ring stretching vibrations are displayed at 1515.2 and 1590.6 cm−1. The characteristic absorption in the range of 2237.4 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of the nitrile group. Also, the decrease in vibrational frequency of the nitrile group from 2250.0 to 2237.4 cm−1 is due to the conjugation. In the FT-IR spectrum of NCQDs [16,27], the broad absorption peak in region 3212.3 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibrations of the -OH group (alcohol and acid) and -N-H group (amine); these vibrations overlap. Stretching vibrations related to -C-H aliphatic are shown at 2935.4 cm−1. The absorption peak at 1712.7 cm−1 is attributed to an acid carbonyl group. Due to hydrogen bonding, the frequency of the acidic carbonyl group has decreased from 1760.0 to 1712.7 cm−1 [28]. The absorption frequency shown in the region of 1651.7 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the bond -C꞊C. Vibration peaks at 1100.4 cm−1 were attributed to the functional group -C-O (alcohol) and 1286.0 cm−1 to the functional group -C-O (carboxylic acid). Characteristic absorption at the frequency of 1385.5 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibrations of the -C-N amine bond or the symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylate salt carbonyl group [29,30]. Absorption peaks related to symmetric stretching vibrations at frequency 1251.0 cm−1 and peaks related to asymmetric stretching vibrations at frequencies 959.4 and 786.8 cm−1 are related to the -C-OC bond of epoxy. Stretching vibrations of the -C-N bond of aromatic and aliphatic amines are in the area of 1350.0 and 1182.7 cm−1, respectively [27,31]. As a result, these peaks confirm nitrogen doping in the CQD [23,32]. In the FT-IR spectrum of the N, Cl-CQD absorption peak at the frequency of 1584.8 cm−1 shows the stretching vibration of the carbonyl group of the amide. Conjugation is the cause of the decrease in the vibration frequency of the amide carbonyl group from 1630.0 to 1584.8 cm−1. Stretching vibration of the -C-N bond of the first type amide is visible in frequency1406.3 cm−1. Vibration frequency in 1090.3 cm−1 was assigned to the group -C-O. Absorption peaks at positions 3020.1 and 953.1 cm−1 show stretching and bending vibrations of the ꞊CH bond (aromatic), respectively. Stretching vibration of the -C-N bond of aromatic and aliphatic amines is shown at 1347.2 and 1189.1 cm−1, respectively. The -N-H bond of the aromatic amine of the second type is responsible for the strong absorption at 3256.0 cm−1. The peak at 591.8 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibrations of the -C-Cl bond. As a result, these peaks confirm the doping of nitrogen and chlorine in the CQD [23,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 1H nmr and 13C nmr analysis study of dyes 3c and 3e
Photoluminescence and UV–Vis absorption spectrums study
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–Vis spectrum of the synthesized dyes (3a-3e) is shown in Fig. 2
a. Synthesized dyes are known to have a maximum absorption in the visible range of 415 to 448 nm [33,34], which is due to the electronic transitions (n → π*) of the C = N bonds of the pyrazine ring [33–35]. The dyes 3b and 3d show intense absorption peaks in the area (448 and 436 nm) and weaker absorption peaks at wavelengths 590 and 550 nm. Corresponding peaks with lower intensity at around 500 nm are related to intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [34]. The UV–Vis spectrum of NCQDs is shown in Fig. 2b. The NCQDs show a superior absorption band around 250 and 344 nm. The absorption peak at 250 nm is attributed to the (π→π*) transition of aromatic ring C꞊C bonds [31]. While the broad absorption peak in area 344 nm could correspond to an (n→π*) transition of C꞊O bonds [36,16]. The UV–Visible spectrum of N, Cl-CQD is shown in Fig. 2c. The N, Cl-CQD have two absorption peaks at 239 and 336 nm. The absorption band at 239 nm is attributed to the (π→π*) transition of C꞊C bonds from the aromatic ring, and the absorption band at 336 nm is associated with the (n→π*) transition of C = O bond [23]. Under the illumination of a UV lamp (λex=365 nm), aqueous soluble doped-CQDs emit blue fluorescence (inset of Fig 2b and 2c) [16,23]. Photoluminescence spectroscopy analysis (PL) of NCQD and N, Cl-CQD is shown in Fig. 2 d. The fluorescence emission wavelength of doped-CQDs is located at 420 nm (λex=365 nm), which shows the fluorescence nature of the quantum dots [16,23].

The XRD analysis of NCQD and N, Cl-CQD was done in a 2θ range of 10°–80° and shown in Fig. 3
(a, b). The XRD patterns of the NCQD and N, Cl-CQD show a broad diffraction peak at 19.61° and 22.95°, respectively, which is attributed to disordered carbon atoms [28,37]. The XRD pattern of the N, Cl-CQD shows the diffraction peaks at 23°, 33°, and 58°, which are attributed to the indices of 110, 210, and 212 of urea, respectively [38].

The surface topography of the NCQD and N, Cl-CQD by atomic force microscopy is depicted. The typical 2D, 3D, and height profile images are shown in Fig. 4 (a, b). The obtained images show that doped-CQDs are shaped in round disks [39].

The DLS analysis of the doped-carbon quantum dots is shown in Fig. 5 . The average particle sizes for NCQDs and N, Cl-CQDs obtained are 0.69 nm and 0.65 nm, respectively. Results show that synthesized quantum dots have particles smaller than 10 nm.

Figs. 6 (a, b) and (c, d) depict the morphology and nanostructure details of NCQDs and N, Cl-CQDs, respectively. The images demonstrate an even dispersion without aggregation. According to statistics, NCQD has a diameter of approximately 9 nm (Fig. 6e). Also, Fig. 6f depicts the predicted lateral size distribution of the N, Cl-CQDs with an average diameter of 8 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and elemental mapping analysis study
ce:section.ce:para: Using an energy EDX spectrometer, the elemental compounds of NCQD and N, Cl-CQD were qualitatively analyzed. Fig. 7 depicts the EDX and mapping of the respective elements. For NCQD, the measurements show that the amount of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen obtained for the adjusted laser processing condition is 62.25, 22.44, and 15.31 wt %, respectively ( Fig. 7 a) [40]. The elements C, N, and O of NCQD are depicted in the relevant elemental mapping ( Fig. 7 b, c, d, and e), respectively. The carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and chlorine amounts obtained for N, Cl-CQD are 44.36, 20.12, 34.18, and 1.34 wt %, respectively ( Fig. 7 f) [40,41]. The corresponding elemental mapping ( Fig. 7 g, h, i, j, and k) shows that the synthesized N, Cl-CQD contains C, N, O, and Cl elements, respectively (Table 1).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) is one of the most potent and vital techniques for studying compound oxidation and reduction reactions. CV measurements were performed in a nitrogen atmosphere (N2), and the electrolyte solution and dye were aerated for 10 min. The electrodes are glassy carbon, Ag/AgCl, and platinum wire, which are used as working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively. For cyclic voltammetry measurement, synthesized dyes with (0.5 mM/DMF), and lithium perchlorate solution (0.1 M) were used as a supporting electrolyte. CV of synthesized dyes (3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) with scan rates (50 and 100 mV/s) is shown in Fig. 8 . In anode and cathode sweeps, one reversible peak of oxidation–reduction was observed for dye 3a with E1/2 values of −0.093 and −0.123 V, respectively. Also, one quasi-reversible peak related to oxidation–reduction was recorded in anode and cathode sweeps for dye 3a, with E1/2 values of −0.58 and −0.76 V, respectively. The dye 3b shows a quasi-reversible behavior, and the E1/2 values in the anode and cathode sweeps were (0.06, −0.67 V) and (−0.10, −0.77 V), respectively. The dye 3c shows a quasi-reversible behavior, and the E1/2 values in the anode and cathode sweeps are (0.06, −0.50 V) and (−0.20, −0.64 V), respectively. The dye 3d exhibits a well-symmetrical and quasi-reversible behavior, and the E1/2 values in the anode and cathode sweeps are −0.63, and −1.02 V, respectively. The dye 3e shows a quasi-reversible behavior, with two oxidation peaks and one reduction peak in the anode and cathode sweeps, and the E1/2 values are 0.075, −0.515 V, and −1.04 V, respectively. As a result of investigating the electrochemical behavior of the dyes, we concluded that the electron transfer process is quasi-reversible in the synthesized dyes (3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e).

As depicted in Scheme 4 , the reversible reduction mechanism of phenazines in aprotic solvents involves two successive one-electron reduction steps to form first the radical anion and the dianion in the second step [42,43]. For phenazine derivatives (3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d), quasi-reversible reduction peaks were obtained at negative potentials. Quasi-reversible cyclic voltammetry for many phenazine derivatives has been reported in references [44–47]. The ease of reduction for phenazine derivatives (3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) can be rationalized partly by intramolecular H-bonding of the hydroxyl group with N-pyrazine [47].

For quinoxaline derivative (3e), one cathodic peak (Ic) was obtained at Ep, Ic= −1.04 V. One main anodic peak (Ia) appeared at Ep, Ia= −0.515 V, and one additional anodic peak (IIa) at potential Ep, IIa= +0.075 V were noticed. According to the presented in the references, it seems that the reduction of quinoxaline occurs as one-step quasi-reversible reaction in acid and neutral medium, reaching reversibility in alkaline solutions, always involving the transfer of two electrons and two protons [48]. The proposed mechanism is presented in the Scheme 5 . The appearance of the anodic peaks Ia and IIa corresponds to the oxidation of the quinoxaline: peak Ia is a consequence of the re-oxidation to quinoxaline, and peak IIa is due to the formation of a hydroxy‑derivative, which is present only in acid and neutral solutions. According to Scheme 5, an intermediary hydroxy‑derivative (that would spontaneously isomerize to the more stable lactam-tautomer) is formed in a one-electron irreversible reaction. The formation of this product has been reported for some compounds with similar structures [48–51].

Cyclic voltammetry is one of the most useful methods to characterize organic materials and estimate the energy band diagram. For organic semiconductors, HOMO indicates the energy required to extract an electron from a molecule, which is an oxidation process, and LUMO is the energy necessary to inject an electron into a molecule, thus implying a reduction process [52]. The HOMO energy level was calculated using the Bredas empirical equation and according to equation E (HOMO) = -e [Eox onset + 4.4] [52,53]. The longest absorption wavelength λonset was used to calculate the optical gap energy (Egap), according to the formula Egap = 1242/λonset [52,54,55]. The LUMO energy level was calculated using the equation E (LUMO)= EHOMO+Egap [53]. The HOMO and LUMO levels obtained from the CV as well as the optical band gap are shown in Table 2 . For charge transfer in a DSSC, the energy levels of the sensitizer, TiO2, and I −/I − 3 must match each other. All derivatives here proposed have frontier orbitals with the appropriate alignment of energy levels than the conduction band of TiO2 (−4.0 eV) and the HOMO energy level of the I −/I − 3 electrolyte (−4.8 eV). So, the LUMO energy levels of dyes (−2.72, −2.81, −2.41, −2.60, and −2.40 eV) are higher than the LUMO of TiO2, as a result, electron injection into the TiO2 layer is quickly done. Also, the HOMO energy levels of the dyes (−5.30, −5.24, −5.07, −5.20, and −5.12 eV) are lower than the HOMO of I −/I − 3 electrolyte, so dyes oxidized are quickly restored. The results obtained from CV show that the synthesized dyes have the potential to be used in DSSCs.

In this work, all computations with the Gaussian 09 package and GaussView 5.0 were done [56,57]. The ground state geometry of all the synthesized structures is optimized by density functional theory (DFT) along with the correlation functions of B3LYP (Becke-Lee-Yang-Parr) and 6–31 G (d) as the basis set at the gas phase [58]. In the following, since the absorption phenomenon is time-dependent, for obtaining the absorption properties, time-dependent-DFT (TD-DFT) calculations were done [58,59]. Some of the essential parameters such as (HOMO/LUMO) shape and energy level, gap band energy (Egap), absorption spectrum, oscillator strengths (OS), vertical excitation energy (Eex), main transition (MO), molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) maps, and photovoltaic parameters like open-circuit voltage (Voc) employing DFT and TD-DFT methods in the theory level of theory B3LYP/6–31G(d) were studied and investigated [56,60]. The Multiwfn program does intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) of dyes in the optimized ground state [61,62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimized ground state geometry of synthesized dyes
ce:section.ce:para:
The optimized structure of the dyes is given in Fig. 9 . The bond length range obtained for the synthesized dyes is as follows: CC: 1.38–1.44 Å, CN: 1.32–1.35 Å, C ≡ N: 1.15 Å CO: 1.2 Å, N
O: 1.23 Å, CO: 1.35 Å, CC: 1.46–1.5 Å, CH: 1.08 Å, and CN: 1.47 Å, which indicates a delocalized π-system. Therefore, all dyes have a planar structure, which can facilitate intramolecular charge transfers (ICT) [56,63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum chemical properties of synthesized dyes
ce:section.ce:para: The main characteristic of synthesized organic compounds is an intramolecular charge transition (ICT). Charge transfer in the pull-push architecture of organic structures is from the donor part to the acceptor and is done via the π bridge [56]. Therefore, for charge transfer in a DSSC, the energy levels of the sensitizer, semiconductor, and electrolyte must match each other. The LUMO energy level of the sensitizer should be higher than the conduction band of TiO2 (−4.0 eV) to provide the necessary driving forces for electron injection into the semiconductor conduction band. Also, the HOMO energy levels of the sensitizer should be lower than the HOMO energy level of the I −/I − 3 electrolyte (−4.8 eV); under these conditions, molecules that have lost electrons can be retrieved by obtaining electrons from the electrolyte [64,65]. The molecular orbitals energy (HOMO and LUMO) and the band gap (Egap) of all structures obtained by calculation are given in Table 3 .

To have a generic vision of the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), an energy-level diagram of the dyes, electrolyte (I −/I − 3), and semiconductor is depicted in Fig. 10 . As in the diagram shown, the LUMO energy levels of dyes are higher than the LUMO of TiO2, as a result, electron injection into the TiO2 layer is quickly done. Also, the HOMO energy levels of the dyes are lower than HOMOelectrolyte, so dyes oxidized are quickly restored. The value Egap for synthesized dyes is as follows: 5a > 1a > 4a > 3a > 2a. A smaller energy gap causes a faster transfer of excited electrons. As a result, this feature can help to increase short circuit current (Jsc) and efficiency. As shown in Table 3, dye 3b has the lowest Egap.

The FMOs of the dyes are shown in Fig 11; this helps to understand the electron density distribution of the frontier orbitals and indicates the charge separation between the bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals. The photo-stimulation of the sensitizer is an essential factor in the working process of DSSC. The molecular orbitals (HOMO) and (LUMO) take part in the photo-stimulation process [67]. As shown in the Fig. 11 , HOMO and LUMO have π-conjugated characteristics. As can be observed, the HOMO of all dyes is localized on the whole molecular skeleton, but functional groups of -CH3, -NO2, and -CO-pH are uncontributed in HOMO. In other words, the bonding molecular orbital of dyes 3d and 3e are entirely in whole skeleton molecular delocalized. Also, the LUMO of all dyes is localized on the entire molecular frame, but the functional group of CH3 is uncontributed in LUMO. In other words, the antibonding molecular orbitals of dyes 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e are entirely in whole skeleton molecular delocalized. Therefore, in all dyes, the distribution of electrons in an antibonding molecular orbital is towards anchor groups such as nitro, carbonyl, nitrile, and pyrazine ring. The results show good conditions for electron separation between HOMO and LUMO orbitals. These conditions cause the better absorption of dyes on the surface of TiO2 and the injection of more electrons into the CB of TiO2, thus increasing the efficiency [57,68,69].

The TD-DFT analyses for obtaining the UV–Vis spectra of dyes were performed. Calculations in DMF solvent and at B3LYP/6–31G(d) level of theory are done. The presence of an absorption band of (visible and near-infrared area) solar radiation provides energy harvesting of sunlight [70]. Essential factors in light harvesting are as follows: maximum wavelength (λmax), vertical excitation energy (Eex), oscillating strength (f), and transition properties for the original absorption (MO). These parameters were studied for all dyes, and their values are given in Table 4 [70]. As shown in Table 4, all synthesized dyes at experimental and theoretical conditions demonstrate an intense peak in the visible light region between 415 and 448 and 399–476 nm, respectively. Compared to other dyes, dye 3b in the results obtained theoretically and experimentally, the broad absorption band shows that this feature is vital for light harvesting [70]. Also, the higher λmax value indicates that the grafted nitro group with dye 3b positive effect has on the absorption band expansion. Another factor that has been studied is the excitation energy (Eex) of dyes. They are listed here in order: 3a (3.10 eV) > 3e (3.09 eV) > 3d (3.06 eV) > 3c (2.85 eV) > 3b (2.60 eV). All these values are lower than the (Egap) of the dyes, thus indicating electron injection into the CB of TiO2 and the regeneration of the sensitizer is feasible [56]. Also, the lowest excitation energy of a compound is attributed to easy charge transfer from the bonding orbital to the antibonding orbital. This property is crucial for charge separation [70]. Therefore, we expect the dye with the lowest excitation energy can increase the light-harvesting potential at higher wavelengths [71]. Values of oscillator strength (f) in Table 4 are given. This parameter indicates the power of the transition of the dye (for example, from HOMO to LUMO). In other words, superior oscillator strength (f) expresses the outstanding potential of dye as a sensitizer [65]. Therefore, higher values of f indicate the dye's higher capability in light harvesting [70]. Also, as shown in Table 4, all dyes have a main transition in the visible region from HOMO to LUMO. Another parameter being studied is related to the photovoltaic characteristic (Voc). The relationship between LUMO and the Voc of the dyes studied depends on electron injection from LUMOdye to CB of TiO2 [56]. To analyze the relationship between them, we used the formula Voc = ELUMO – ECB/e [60,65]. The results show that the values of Voc are in the range of 0.73–1.47 eV in the case of TiO2. These positive results show that electron transfer will be quickly done from the studied materials to the CB of TiO2 [56]. According to the decreasing order, Voc of dyes: 3a > 3d > 3c > 3e > 3b. Perhaps the conjugation extension of dyes is the reason for the decreased Voc of dye that causes aggregation of dyes and π-π stacking interactions. Still, in general, the obtained values indicate efficient electron injection into the LUMO of the semiconductor [71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) of synthesized dyes
ce:section.ce:para: MEP maps were calculated at the B3LYP/6–31G(d) level of theory to study interaction sites between the semiconductor and the anchor group. Indeed, MEP is a very useful descriptor to determine suitable positions for electrophile-nucleophile attack and intermolecular interactions such as hydrogen bonding, etc. [60]. Diverse colors in the MEP surface indicate different values from electrostatic potential. More negative color red areas point to electron-rich regions, so this area is suitable for electrophilic reactions. Also, more positive color blue areas indicate electron-poor parts, so this area is ideal for nucleophilic reactions [60,69]. MEP map and color code of all dyes are given in Fig. 12 . MEP map for all dyes shows that the hydrogen atom of the hydroxyl group is the preferred site for nucleophilic attack and has the highest positive potential. Also, the most tremendous, negative potential exists on the nitrogen atoms of the pyrazine ring (3a, 3d), the nitrogen atom of the pyrazine ring and nitro group (3b), the nitrogen atom of the pyrazine ring and carbonyl group (3c), and the nitrogen atoms of nitrile (3e), which are the preferred sites for the electrophilic attack. Since electron-efficient injection depends on effective interaction between the sensitizer and the semiconductor surface, according to the MEP map, all the synthesized dyes have coordination sites, making them suitable for absorption on the semiconductor's surface.

A fundamental characteristic of organic dyes is intramolecular charge transfer. This feature plays an essential role in converting light into electricity in DSSC. In DSSCs, the absorption of sunlight through a dye molecule adsorbed on a semiconductor creates an electron-hole. The positive and negative charges must have enough distance to transfer the favorable charge. For this reason, designing a sensitizer with a pull-push structure is profitable for maximizing intramolecular charge transfer [61]. The Multiwfn program was used to analyze charge transfer in dyes. The ∆r parameter, which measures the separation distance of the electron-hole, was used to study the identity of the electron excitation process [72]. The following criteria were used to categorize the transmission type. 1) The excitation process is assumed charge transfer (CT) when ∆r > 2 Å. 2) When ∆r < 2 Å, local excitation (LE) should be considered [72]. Values of ∆r obtained for dyes using the Multiwfn program are as follows: (3a: 1.29 Å), (3b: 3.65 Å), (3c: 2.19 Å), (3d: 1.25 Å), (3e: 1.75 Å). Type of excitation in dyes 3a, 3d, and 3e are of LE, and in dyes 3b, 3c are of CT (Table 5 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectric properties of solar cells sensitized by pure dyes, Dye/N-CQD, and Dye/N, Cl-CQD
ce:section.ce:para:
DSSCs convert light to electricity in the visible spectrum by combining two transparent electrodes. The production of photocurrent occurs at a DSSC according to the mechanism shown in Fig. 13 . DSSCs convert light to electricity by combining two transparent electrodes. Photocurrent production occurs at a DSSC according to the mechanism shown in Fig. 13. The photovoltaic process generally includes four steps: 1) The absorption of a photon from sunlight by the sensitizer (dye) and photoexcitation of the dye from the ground state (S) to the excited state (S*). 2) Electron injection into the conduction band of a semiconductor. 3) The oxidized dye is reduced to its primary neutral state by obtaining an electron from the iodide ion in the redox couple and generating a triiodide ion. 4) The triiodide ion is reduced to the iodide ion by the charge transfer process from the cathode, and the electrolyte is regenerated in the final step [73]. The parameters used to evaluate the photoelectric current in DSSC are as follows: 1) Open circuit voltage (Voc): The maximum applied potential difference in a solar cell when no current flows through it. 2) Short circuit current (Jsc): The obtained current from the solar cell when the applied voltage at the solar cell is zero. 3) Fill factor (FF): Describes the shape of the IV curve, whose higher value indicates a curve rectangular shape and is expressed as an equation (ii). 4) Power conversion efficiency (η): Calculated with equation (iii), and P0 is the incident photon power (P0=100) [74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studying the performance of synthesized dyes (3a-3e) as sensitizer
ce:section.ce:para: In this work for studying the performance of synthesized Dyes (3a-3e) as sensitizers, the photovoltaic performance of cells was investigated by illumination generated by an AM 1.5 solar simulator with a 300 W xenon lamp. The power density of the simulated light was calibrated to (100 mW cm−2) using a typical silicon solar cell. Also, the distance between the light source and the samples (DSSC) is 12 cm, and the temperature of the chamber or substrate is 25 °C. To study the effect of dyes (3a-3e) on cell efficiency, the synthesized dyes were used at a concentration of 0.5 mM in DMF solvent. The J-V characteristics of the solar cells sensitized with dyes (3a-3e) are reported in Fig. 14 , and the cell parameters are brought up in Table 6 . Solar cell sensitized with dye 3b gives more efficiency (0.056 %) than the solar cells sensitized by dyes (3a, 3c, 3d, and 3e) as it has a higher Jsc (0.288 mA cm−2), higher Voc (0.437 V) and a higher FF (0.446). Solar cell sensitized by dye 3e has the lowest efficiencies (0.0041 %) with Jsc (0.061 mA cm−2), Voc (0.218 V), and FF (0.307).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance study of DSSCs sensitized with Dye/N-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: The J-V characteristics of the solar cells sensitized with pure NCQD are reported in Fig S2 and the cell parameters in Table S1. To investigate the effect of NCQD on the efficiency of the dyes used in DSSCs, dye and NCQD were synthesized separately. Then, each of the synthesized dyes (3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) and NCQD were mixed with a weight ratio of 1:1 in DMF and investigated their mixed effect on the efficiency of the present solar cell. The J-V characteristics of the solar cells sensitized with Dye/NCQD are reported in Fig. 15 , and the electrical parameters calculated for the optimum cell are given in Table 7 . Results Table 7 shows that the device sensitized with 3a/NCQD offers a Jsc of 0.568 mA cm−2, a Voc of 0.419 V, an FF of 0. 487, and an η of 0.115 %. As a result, the Jsc, Voc, FF, and η of the 3a/NCQD-based sensitized device are increased by approximately 0.494 mA cm−2, 0.169 V, 0.079, and 0.107 %, respectively, compared to the 3a pure dye-based sensitized device. The Jsc, Voc, FF, and η of the solar cell sensitized by 3b/NCQD are 0.594 mA cm−2, 0.421 V, 0.310, and 0.077 %, respectively. As a result, the device sensitized with 3b/NCQD is improved than the device sensitized with 3b pure dye, and the efficiency is increased by about 0.021 %. Also, the Jsc of the present device is increased by approximately 0.306 mA cm−2 compared to the device sensitized by 3b pure dye. Considering the effect of NCQD on the efficiency of the synthesized dyes, the device sensitized with 3c/NCQD shows the highest photocurrent and its photoelectric parameters are given here: 0.979 mA cm−2, 0.465 V, 0.370, and 0.168 %, in that order. Compared to 3c pure dye-based DSSC, the Jsc of the solar cell using 3c/NCQD sensitizer is increased from 0.052 mA cm−2 to 0.979 mA cm−2, Voc is increased from 0.335 V to 0.465 V, and η is increased from 0.0073 % to 0.168 %. For 3d/NCQD-based devices, the photovoltaic parameters are 0.368 mA cm−2, 0.421 V, 0.324, and 0.050 %, respectively. The Jsc, Voc, and η of 3d/NCQD-based DSSC than sensitized device with 3d pure dye are increased by about 0.083 mA cm−2, 0.109 V, and 0.015 %, respectively. Photovoltaic parameters containing Jsc, Voc, FF, and η of the 3e/NCQD-based device are 0.488 mA cm−2, 0.406 V, 0.393, and 0.077 %, respectively. The Jsc, Voc, FF, and η of the present device than 3e pure dye-based device are increased by about 0.427 mA cm−2, 0.188 V, 0.086, and 0.073 %, respectively. Compare the results of Tables 6 and 7 shows that the overall power conversion efficiency is improved for the solar cells sensitized with (3a/NCQD, 3b/NCQD, 3c/NCQD, 3d/NCQD, 3e/NCQD) than dyes (3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 3e) free of NCQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studying the performance DSSCs sensitized with Dye/N, Cl-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: The J-V characteristics of the solar cells sensitized with pure N, Cl-CQD are reported in Fig S2, and the cell parameters in Table S1. To investigate the effect of N, Cl-CQD on the efficiency of the dyes used in DSSCs, dye and N, Cl-CQD were synthesized separately. Then, each of the synthesized dyes (3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) and N, Cl-CQD were mixed with a weight ratio of 1:1 in DMF and investigated their mixed effect on the efficiency of the present solar cell. The J-V characteristics of the solar cells sensitized with Dye/N, Cl-CQD in Fig. 16 , and the electrical parameters for the optimum cell are given in Table 8 . Results Table 8 shows that the device sensitized with 3a/N, Cl-CQD offers a Jsc of 0.241 mA cm−2, a Voc of 0.406 V, an FF of 0. 341, and an η of 0.034 %. As a result, the Jsc, Voc, and η of the 3a/N, Cl-CQD-based sensitized device are increased by approximately 0.167 mA cm−2, 0.156 V, and 0.0265 %, respectively, compared to the 3a pure dye-based sensitized device. The 3b/N, Cl-CQD-based DSSC shows Jsc (0.595 mA cm−2), Voc (0.394 V), FF (0.393), and η (0.092 %). Therefore, the values of Jsc and η, approximately 0.307 mA cm−2 and 0.036 %, are increased compared to 3b pure dye-based DSSC. Photovoltaic results of short-circuit photocurrent density (Jsc) from 0.052 mA cm−2 to 1.31 mA cm−2, open-circuit voltage (Voc) from 0.335 V to 0.495 V, fill factor (FF) from 0.419 to 0.466, and η from 0.0073 % to 0.303 % are increased in the presence of the 3c/N, Cl-CQD sensitizer. Photovoltaic parameters of the device sensitized with 3d/N, Cl-CQD were obtained 0.822 mA cm−2 (Jsc), 0.500 V (Voc), 0.331 (FF), and 0.136 % (η). Compared to 3d pure dye-based DSSC, the Jsc of the solar cell using 3d/N, Cl-CQD sensitizer is increased from 0.285 mA cm−2 to 0.822 mA cm−2, Voc is increased from 0.312 V to 0.500 V and η is increased from 0.035 % to 0.136 %. Results Table 8 shows that the device sensitized with 3e/N, Cl-CQD offers Jsc of 0.397 mA cm−2, Voc of 0.437 V, FF of 0.352, and η of 0.061 %. As a result, the Jsc, Voc, FF, and η of the 3e/N, Cl-CQD-based sensitized device are increased by approximately 0.336 mA cm−2, 0.219 V, 0.045, and 0.057 %, respectively, compared to the 3e pure dye-based sensitized device. When the results of Tables 6 and 8 are compared it is clear that solar cells sensitized with (3a/N, Cl-CQD), (3b/N, Cl-CQD), (3c/N, Cl-CQD), (3d/N, Cl-CQD), and 3e/N, Cl-CQD) have higher overall power conversion efficiency than dyes (3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 3e) free of N, Cl-CQD.

Dye is co-adsorbed with CQD to increase the amount of charge injected into the CB of TiO2 [17,75]. As a result, the performance of the sensitized devices by (Dye/NCQD) and (Dye/N, Cl-CQD) is improved compared to the devices sensitized by pure dye. Also, a dye/QD should be composed of more functional groups (FGs) to act as linkers for a suitable attachment to TiO2 [17,76]. Photovoltaic results show that the dye 3c with functional groups such as two nitrogen pyridines, hydroxyl, and carbonyl are co-adsorbed with NCQD, and N, Cl-CQD has a suitable attachment to TiO2. So, the number of electrons injected into the CB of TiO2 increases. As a result, the device sensitized by 3c/NCQD shows better performance than the devices sensitized by (3a/NCQD, 3b/NCQD, 3d/NCQD, and 3e/NCQD). Also, the device sensitized by 3c/N, Cl-CQD exhibit better performance than the devices sensitized by (3a/N, Cl-CQD, 3b/N, Cl-CQD, 3d/N, Cl-CQD, and 3e/N, Cl-CQD). On the other hand, the device sensitized with 3c/N, Cl-CQD has more efficiency than the device sensitized with 3c/NCQD, which shows the effect of chlorine.

Recent studies have shown that organic sensitizers with pull-push configurations have the best efficiency in the field of solar cells. They include a donor part (D), an acceptor part (A), and a conjugated linker. In this system, directional electron transfer from the donor part to the acceptor part facilitates efficient electron injection. Usually, the acceptor part contains an anchor group to bind the sensitizer to TiO2. Also, pull-push compounds displayed intense charge transfer; as a result, the extension of the absorption band to the infrared region significantly increases light harvesting yield [77]. The most commonly used anchor group in DSSCs is acid carboxylic and acid cyanoacrylic groups. Dye molecules are assembled on a metal oxide substrate through covalent bonding, hydrophobic interaction, electrostatic interaction, van der Waals bonding, hydrogen bonding, etc. DSSCs exclusively use covalent bonding to absorb the dye on the TiO2 surface to ensure strong (dye/TiO2) coupling, dye homogenous distribution, and device stability. But other interactions have weaker bonds, and usually, dye adsorption is pseudo-reversible and unstable. Possible anchor models: bidentate bridging, bidentate chelating, mono and bi-dentate hydrogen bonding, and monodentate coordinating state that by a carbonyl group done [78,79]. Attachment of a -CN group to the π-bridge of a conjugated compound expands the absorption region of organic dyes. Also, the added -CN group improves the electron-withdrawing capability of the electron acceptor moiety [80,81]. According to previous studies, many compounds with different anchor groups have been investigated in the DSSC [78,79,82]. The nitro group is a novel anchoring group in DSSCs. The presence of a nitro group and carboxylic acid in a compound has shown that the nitro group causes the sensitization behavior and surface adsorption phenomenon on TiO2 substrates. Also, coordination of the nitro group with the TiO2 surface causes optical bleaching of the light absorption, increases the TiO2-dye coupling, and, as a result, easier electron transfer [78,83]. Therefore, according to the explanations mentioned about the functional groups as anchors and based on the results obtained experimentally and theoretically, the current-voltage curves were discussed for the synthesized dyes. In this study, the role of electron-withdrawing and electron-donating substituents such as (-CH3, -NO2, -CO, -CN, and -OH) on phenazine and quinoxaline central cores was investigated to find the ideal dye as a sensitizer. Nitro, nitrile, and carbonyl substituents are electron-withdrawing groups with resonance. The electron-withdrawing ability of the anchor groups in the para position is according to the Hammett reference value as follows: -NO2 (σ = 0.78) > −CN (σ = 0.66) > −CHO (σ= 0.22) [78]. The development of electron-deficient rings is a result of these functional groups. Hydroxyl and methyl groups are resonance and inductive electron-donating functional groups, respectively. Due to the hyperconjugation (σ-conjugation), the methyl substituent is a weak electron-donating group [7]. The J-V curves of the synthesized dyes are presented in Fig. 14 and Table 6. According to the obtained data, dye 3b has higher efficiency. The electron density distribution results from frontier orbitals show that the LUMO of dye 3b is localized on the nitro group and aromatic ring nitrogen atoms of pyrazine. On the other hand, pull-push structures usually have intense charge transfer. As a result, they show broad absorption bands due to charge transfer bands [77]. These properties can be seen in the experimental and theoretical absorption spectrum obtained for dye 3b. Also, the calculation results show dye 3b has the lowest Egap (Egap=3.08 eV). An ideal Egap of a compound is beneficial for light absorption at long wavelengths [84]; it increases the interaction between the donor-acceptor groups of the combination [85] and also facilitates the transfer of excited electrons [86]. So, dye 3b, which has the smallest Egap among the investigated dyes, can absorb light at a longer wavelength. Also, dye 3b has low excitation energy (Eex=2.60 eV); this value is favorable for electron injection and charge separation [65]. The results of the charge distribution of frontier orbitals show good conditions for electron separation and the injection of more electrons into the CB of TiO2. The electrostatic potential map shows that the nitro group and nitrogen of the pyrazine ring are preferred coordination sites of dye with TiO2 [60,69]. The Multiwfn program was used to analyze the value of ∆r to make it possible to distinguish between LE or CT processes. Based on the obtained result, the ∆r is calculated to be 3.65 Å for the dye 3b; it means that the structure has a charge transfer process. Therefore, the experimental and theoretical results are in perfect agreement with each other and confirm dye 3b with values of Jsc (0.288 mA cm−2), Voc (0.437 V), FF (0.446), η (0.056 %) is the best sensitizer compared to other dyes. Results obtained from the charge distribution of the frontier orbitals show that dye 3c has a favorable charge separation. Also, the electrostatic potential map shows the carbonyl group and nitrogen of the pyrazine ring are suitable coordination sites for dye 3c [60,69]. Furthermore, dye 3c has fewer (Egap= 3.34 eV) and (Eex= 2.85 eV) than dyes 3a, 3d, and 3e. The calculated ∆r value for dye 3c is 2.16 Å, indicating that the structure has a charge transfer process. For sensitized solar cell with dye 3c was obtained photovoltaic values Jsc (0.052 mA cm−2), Voc (0.335 V), FF (0.419), and η (0.0073 %). The comparison of results of dyes 3a and 3d shows that there is charge separation between their frontier orbitals. The electrostatic potential map shows that dyes 3a and 3d can coordinate on the surface of TiO2 by nitrogen atoms of the pyrazine ring [60,69]. Also, their Eex and Egap show the same values with a slight difference of approximately 0.04 eV. The ∆r values for dyes 3a and 3d are calculated to be 1.29 Å and 1.25 Å, respectively. This value demonstrates the presence of a local excitation process in the compositions. However, the experimental results show that dye 3d has higher efficiency than dyes 3a, 3c, and 3e. The UV–Visible results show that the dye 3d has a broad absorption spectrum compared to dyes 3a, 3c, and 3e. Broad absorption bands are vital for light harvesting [70]. As a result, photovoltaic values Jsc (0.285 mA cm−2), Voc (0.312 V), FF (0.395), and (0.035 %) were obtained for the device sensitized with dye 3d Photovoltaic values Jsc (0.074 mA cm−2), Voc (0.250 V), FF (0.408), and (0.0075 %) were obtained for the DSSC sensitized with dye 3a. The results obtained from the charge distribution of the frontier orbitals show that dye 3e has a charge separation. The electrostatic potential map shows that the nitrogen atoms of the nitrile group are possible coordination sites of the dye on the surface of TiO2 [60,69]. Since the nitrile group is a strong electron-withdrawing anchor group, we expect that more dye will be adsorbed on the TiO2 surface and the efficiency will increase, but the experimental results are contrary to expectations. Also, according to the MEP map, the electron-withdrawing properties of the nitrile group cause a reduction of the electron density of nitrogen atoms of the pyrazine ring. As a result, a weaker interaction between the dye and TiO2 layer is suggested. It causes a lesser absorption of dye on the surface of the TiO2 layer. According to the calculations, dye 3e has the highest Egap (3.67 eV) than other dyes. The position of the absorption band of a sensitizer has an intimate relationship with Egap [86]. An ideal energy gap is helpful for more light harvesting and easier electron excitation [84]. As a result, dye molecules can absorb more photons and have higher Jsc and η [84]. So, dye 3e, which has the highest Egap among the investigated dyes, is not beneficial for absorbing light. The Eex value of dye 3e (3.09 eV) was obtained. The UV–visible spectrum of dye 3e indicates an absorption peak in a lower wavelength than other dyes (3a, 3b, 3c, 3d). The calculated ∆r value for dye 3e is 1.75 Å, indicating that the structure has a local excitation process. The experimental results show that dye 3e has the lowest efficiency with Jsc (0.061 mA cm−2), Voc (0.218 V), FF (0.307) and, η (0.0041 %). To enhance efficiency, the photoanode of a solar cell can be modified using carbon nanostructures [87]. Carbon nanomaterials (CQDs) have features such as a large specific surface, excellent ability to absorb ultraviolet light and convert it to visible light, high electron mobility, higher extinction coefficient, good chemical stability, non-toxicity, good solubility, the ability to adjust the band gap, and the production of multiple excitons, which increases the efficiency of QDSCs [87–89]. Dopant addition is another way to modify the electrical and photophysical properties of CQDs, and it depends on the electronegativity of the elements. Carbone materials doped with S and Se atoms, which are less electronegative, exhibit a red-shift, while those doped with N, O, and F exhibit a blue shift [17,90]. The excellent optical properties of the NCQDs are related to the layered structure of the NCQDs, which creates a superior conjugated system containing delocalized π electrons [91]. NCQDs can reduce recombination resistance and prevent back electron transfer to redox species. Also, their attachment capacity on the TiO2 surface can be improved by GQD-functional with amino groups and decreased interfacial resistance at the N-GQD/TiO2 interface [17,76]. The unpaired electrons of halogen atoms show both resonance donation and electron withdrawal due to an electro-negativity-type phenomenon [92]. The Yang team incorporated chlorine with carbon compounds in 2013 [93]. Combining dye molecules with CQDs as supracollector nanocomposite materials provides an additional control step over their charge transfer and recombination characteristics [75]. Carbon nanomaterials added to the organic dye molecule expand the dye's absorption spectrum, thus improving the dye's light-harvesting efficiency [94–97]. Furthermore, it reduces charge recombination, increases the concentration of charge carriers accessible in the photoreactions, and facilitates separation and charge transfer behavior in DSSCs [98–100]. The first condition for this approach to be efficient is to follow the type-II alignment. Dye has two roles: (i) efficient light absorption in the visible spectrum and (ii) hole scavenger agent that induces separation of hole-electron pairs in CQD. Dyes (as acceptors) receive positively charged holes from the HOMOCQD (as donors) [17,75]. The mismatch between the LUMODye and LUMOCQD surfaces creates a stepped energy band and helps the electrons of the LUMODye surface be injected into the CB of TiO2 through the LUMOCQD surface [87]. On the other hand, the CQD between the TiO2 and dye limits the injected electron's ability to recombine back with the redox couple and also limits the electron-hole recombination in the HOMODye [87]. Given the beneficial effect of carbon nanomaterials on the mechanism of electron transfer and recombination in DSSCs, the use of doped carbon quantum dots (NCQD and N, Cl-CQD) in this work improves the efficiency of solar cells sensitized by dye/NCQD and dye/N, Cl-CQD compared to solar cells sensitized by dye free of doped-CQD.

Five metal-free organic dyes have been developed based on the phenazine and quinoxaline derivatives (3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e). These dyes have a pull-push nature, and their usability as a sensitizer in DSSCs is studied. These organic dyes exhibit a photo-spectral response in the visible range. Due to the presence of an electron-rich group, such as oxygen, and a group with an intense electron deficiency, i.e., the nitro group, and the existence of conjugate π bonds between these two groups, the dye 3b has the highest donor-acceptor characteristics. As a result, the greater the electron-donating strength and the greater the electron-withdrawing strength, the easier the electron current between these two systems is, and absorption occurs at longer wavelengths. Results of the UV–Vis showed that dye 3b has a broader spectrum than other dyes. The J-V curve shows that DSSCs sensitized by 3b dye with Jsc (0.288 mA cm−2), Voc (0.437 V), FF (0.446), and η (0.056 %) have the best performance. The current study demonstrated that using N, Cl-doped carbon quantum dots can improve the efficiency of sensitized solar cells with phenazine and quinoxaline derivatives. We investigated the effect of co-doping on DSSC performance improvement. Two sets of dye/doped-CQD systems, including dye/NCQD and dye /N, Cl-CQD, are provided to investigate the effect of doping quantum dots with heteroatoms on the performance of DSSCs. It is known: 1) Nitrogen and carbon combine effortlessly in carbon-based compounds due to their similar atomic sizes; 2) Electronegativity differences in N and C cause polarization in the carbon structure and stimulate the electron; and 3) the charge carrier density in NCQD increased with nitrogen insertion into the carbon structure, which is due to intense conjugation between the π-system of carbon-based materials and the unpaired electrons of nitrogen atoms. Because of these factors, the NCQD improves the device's performance. As a result, the performance of sensitized solar cells with dye/NCQD is better than sensitized solar cells with dye free of NCQD. As previously noted, chlorine improves charge transfer in the compound, thus increasing device performance. This feature is due to the donating and withdrawing capabilities of chlorine. As a result, the efficiency of sensitized solar cells with dye/N, Cl-CQD is better than sensitized solar cells with dye free of N, Cl-CQD. Also, the functional groups of dye 3c, such as carbonyl, nitrogen atoms of the pyrazine ring, and hydroxyl, are acceptor-donors of the hydrogen bond, and it also has a more conjugated system. Therefore, the results show that the 3c/N, Cl-CQD sensitizer-based DSSC with photovoltaic parameters of Jsc (1.31 mA cm−2), Voc (0.495 V), FF (0.466), and η (0.303 %) has better performance than the 3c/NCQD sensitizer-based DSSC with photovoltaic parameters Jsc (0.979 mA cm−2), Voc (0.465 V), FF (0.370), and η (0.168 %). So N, Cl-CQD have better potential to improve the performance of DSSCs than NCQDs.
Shabnam Jahanbani: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Rahim Ghadari: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
